scene.mamba.title: Mamba — selective copying
mamba.ch.intro.title: The task
mamba.ch.intro.body: Selective copying is a memory test. The first 16 positions hide 4 data tokens (ids 1–8) among NOISE tokens (id 9); then a GO marker (id 10); then the model must reproduce the 4 data tokens, in their original order. To win it has to notice and store each data token as it streams past, skip the noise, and hold what it stored until GO. Every cell on screen is a real number from a 2-layer Mamba (d_model 48) that solves this at over 99% accuracy — nothing here is mocked.
mamba.ch.embed.title: Embedding
mamba.ch.embed.body: Each of the 21 token ids looks up one row of the embedding table E (16 ids × 48 features) and copies it into the residual stream — the tall 21 × 48 grid down the middle that every later stage reads from and writes back to. From here on the tokens are gone; the model works entirely with these continuous 48-dimensional vectors. Hover any cell to see its exact value and the row and column it came from. The glowing thread down the centre is that residual stream — the spine the whole computation hangs on.
mamba.ch.block.title: The block
mamba.ch.block.body: A Mamba block reads the residual stream, computes a correction, and adds it back — it never overwrites, only edits. The sequence is: RMSNorm rescales each position; in_proj expands 48 → 192 and splits it into a value path x and a gate z; a depthwise causal conv mixes each channel over the last few positions; the selective SSM scans along time; the gate z filters the result; and out_proj projects 96 → 48 to write the correction back. The matrices beside each stage are the learned weights it multiplies by — follow the spine to see where each result rejoins the stream.
mamba.ch.conv.title: Local mixing
mamba.ch.conv.body: Before the long-range scan, a depthwise 1-D convolution (kernel width 4) lets every channel blend the current position with the previous three. Depthwise means each of the 96 channels has its own small filter — no mixing across channels here. Causal means it only looks backward, never at future tokens, so the model stays a valid left-to-right predictor. This is cheap, local context; remembering a data token from 15 steps ago is the scan's job, not the conv's.
mamba.ch.selection.title: Selection
mamba.ch.selection.body: This stage is the whole point of Mamba, and where it departs from older state-space models. The step size Δ and the scan inputs B and C are not fixed weights — they are computed from the current token, by x_proj and dt_proj. A linear time-invariant SSM reuses one A, B, C at every position, so it treats every token the same. Here Δ, B, C change token by token, which lets the model decide, per token, how much to write into memory and how much to read out. That input-dependence is what 'selective' means — and it is exactly what a copy-and-ignore task needs.
mamba.ch.scan.title: The scan
mamba.ch.scan.body: This is the recurrence that carries memory through time, run one token at a time. The state h is a fixed-size grid (d_inner 96 × d_state 8) — it never grows, however long the sequence. At each step h ← exp(Δ⊙A)·h + Δ⊙B·x: the first term decays the old state, the second writes in the new token, both scaled by the input-dependent Δ. When Δ ≈ 0 the new token is essentially ignored and the old memory passes through intact; when Δ is large the token is written in strongly. Play the scan and watch Δ (layer0.delta.out) stay small on NOISE positions and open up on data tokens — that selective gate is the copying, happening in front of you.
mamba.ch.gate.title: Gate & output
mamba.ch.gate.body: The scan produces an output y, one 96-vector per position. Before it can rejoin the stream it passes a gate: y is multiplied elementwise by SiLU(z), where z is the second half of in_proj from back at the block's start. The gate decides, per channel, how much of the scan's output actually survives. out_proj then projects the gated 96-vector back down to 48 and adds it onto the residual stream — the correction this block contributes.
mamba.ch.layer2.title: Second block
mamba.ch.layer2.body: The residual stream, now carrying the first block's edits, flows into a second identical block — same machine, same shapes, different learned weights. Stacking lets the model build on what the first block found: the first pass might mark which positions are data, the second refine what to emit. Two layers is enough for this toy task; production Mamba models stack dozens.
mamba.ch.readout.title: Readout
mamba.ch.readout.body: After the last block a final RMSNorm rescales the stream, then lm_head projects each 48-vector to 16 logits — one score per vocabulary id. The model's prediction at each position is the argmax of its logits. Look at the four answer positions, just after GO: their argmax picks out the four data tokens, in order. Read them off head.logits and compare to the input — that is the copy, done.
mamba.ch.recap.title: Recap
mamba.ch.recap.body: One fixed-size state, updated through an input-dependent gate, copied four tokens across a noisy sequence — with no attention matrix and no growing cache. Because the state never grows, the work per token stays constant as the sequence lengthens, which is what makes selective state-space models attractive for very long inputs.
scene.gpt.title: GPT — selective copying
gpt.ch.intro.title: The task
gpt.ch.intro.body: GPT is a decoder-only Transformer: at every position it looks back over the earlier tokens with attention and decides what to pull in. Here a 2-layer model (d_model 48, 3 heads) solves selective copying — the input hides 4 data tokens among NOISE filler, then a GO marker, and the model must emit the 4 data tokens in order. Every cell is a real value from a model that solves it at over 99% accuracy; step through to watch attention reach back to the data tokens.
gpt.ch.embed.title: Tokens + positions
gpt.ch.embed.body: GPT adds two embeddings per position: one for the token id, one for the position index. Because attention on its own treats a sequence as an unordered set, the position signal has to be injected explicitly here. Their sum is the residual stream that every block reads from and writes back to.
gpt.ch.block.title: The block
gpt.ch.block.body: A GPT block makes two passes over the residual stream, each a normalize → mix → add-back. First LayerNorm, then self-attention (which moves information between positions); then LayerNorm again, then an MLP (which transforms each position on its own). Neither pass overwrites the stream — both add a correction onto it. Follow the spine: ln1.out, attn.out, ln2.out, mlp.proj are the four stages of this first block.
gpt.ch.qkv.title: Queries, keys, values
gpt.ch.qkv.body: Attention begins by projecting the normalized stream into three roles per position: a query (what am I looking for?), a key (what do I offer?), and a value (what would I pass on?). A single matrix, qkv_proj, produces all three at once; they are then split across 3 heads of 16 dimensions each. q, k, v are shown as three-slab stacks — one slab per head.
gpt.ch.scores.title: Attention scores
gpt.ch.scores.body: Each head compares every query against every key by a dot product, scaled by 1/√d_head: the score matrix S = Q·Kᵀ/√d. Cell [i, j] is how strongly position i wants to read from position j. The strict upper triangle is then set to −∞ so a position can never see the future — that is the dark causal-mask triangle in the scores.
gpt.ch.weights.title: Softmax weights
gpt.ch.weights.body: A softmax over each row turns the raw scores into attention weights: non-negative, summing to 1 — a soft selection of where each position reads from (the masked −∞ cells become exactly 0). Look at the answer rows, just after GO: their weight concentrates on the data-token positions back in the context. That bright band is the copy being set up.
gpt.ch.attnout.title: Attention output
gpt.ch.attnout.body: Each position now takes a weighted average of the value vectors using its attention weights — so an answer position literally pulls in the value of the data token it attended to. The per-head results are concatenated and passed through out_proj, then added back onto the residual stream. The data token has been moved to where it will be emitted.
gpt.ch.mlp.title: MLP
gpt.ch.mlp.body: After attention mixes information across positions, a per-position MLP transforms each token on its own: it expands 48 → 192, applies a GELU nonlinearity, and projects 192 → 48 back into the stream. Every position goes through the same MLP independently — no mixing across positions here. The wide 192-dim hidden layer is where most of the block's parameters live.
gpt.ch.layer2.title: The second block
gpt.ch.layer2.body: The second block repeats the same two passes — attention then MLP — on the now-updated stream, with its own learned weights. It refines what the first block set up: sharpening the copy and cleaning up the representation before readout. Two blocks are enough for this task; deeper GPTs simply stack more of the identical block.
gpt.ch.readout.title: Readout
gpt.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then lm_head projects each position's 48-vector to 16 logits. At the four answer positions the argmax recovers the copied data tokens — attention has carried each one forward from where it appeared to where it must be emitted. Read them off head.logits and compare to the input.
scene.rwkv.title: RWKV — selective copying
rwkv.ch.intro.title: The task
rwkv.ch.intro.body: RWKV is a recurrence that trains in parallel like attention but runs like an RNN. It carries a fixed-size state forward and updates it with a per-channel weighted average of past values that decays over time, plus a gated squared-ReLU MLP — so its cost per token stays constant no matter how long the sequence. Here a 2-layer model (d_model 48) solves selective copying — recover 4 data tokens hidden among NOISE after a GO marker, in order. Every cell is a real value from a model that solves it at over 99% accuracy; watch the decaying state accumulate the data tokens.
rwkv.ch.embed.title: Embedding
rwkv.ch.embed.body: Each token id looks up a row of the embedding table (16 × 48) into the residual stream. RWKV needs no position embedding — order is built into its time recurrence rather than supplied separately. Every block reads this stream and adds a correction back to it.
rwkv.ch.block.title: The block
rwkv.ch.block.body: An RWKV block, like a Transformer block, makes two passes over the residual stream — but it replaces attention with a recurrence. First LayerNorm → time-mixing (RWKV's linear attention, which moves information across positions); then LayerNorm → channel-mixing (a gated MLP per position). Both passes add a correction onto the stream rather than overwriting it. Follow the spine: ln1.out, att.out, ln2.out, ffn.out are the four stages of this first block.
rwkv.ch.rkv.title: Receptance, key, value
rwkv.ch.rkv.body: Time-mixing starts with a token-shift: each position is blended with the one before it by a learned per-channel mix, so a token can mix its own features with its predecessor's. The blended input is then projected three ways — r (receptance, a gate), k (key), v (value) — each its own linear map. r will decide how much of the recurrence's output to let through; k and v feed the decaying state.
rwkv.ch.wkv.title: WKV — the decaying state
rwkv.ch.wkv.body: This is attention without a T×T matrix. The WKV output at position t is a weighted average of all past values v: each earlier value decays by exp(−(t−i)·w) with a per-channel decay w, and the current token gets a bonus u. It runs as a single numerator/denominator state carried forward, so the cost per token is constant no matter the length. Read the decay vector att.0.time_decay: slow-decay channels carry information far down the sequence, fast-decay channels stay local.
rwkv.ch.timeout.title: Gated output
rwkv.ch.timeout.body: The receptance r is squashed to (0,1) by a sigmoid and multiplies the WKV state element-wise — a per-channel gate deciding how much of the recurrence to emit. The gated result is projected by output.weight and added back onto the residual stream: out = (σ(r) ⊙ wkv)·Wo. That is the whole of time-mixing — a recurrence that behaves like attention but costs like an RNN.
rwkv.ch.channelmix.title: Channel-mixing
rwkv.ch.channelmix.body: After time-mixing moves information across positions, the channel-mix transforms each position on its own. A token-shift again, then k = relu(x̂·Wk)² — a squared-ReLU over a wide 192-dim hidden layer — and the result is gated: σ(r) · (k·Wv). This is RWKV's feed-forward block, with a squared-ReLU activation and a receptance gate.
rwkv.ch.layer2.title: The second block
rwkv.ch.layer2.body: The second block repeats the same two passes — time-mixing then channel-mixing — on the now-updated stream, with its own decay rates and weights. With a second recurrence stacked on top, the model can refine what the first carried forward. Deeper RWKVs simply stack more identical blocks; the per-token cost stays flat.
rwkv.ch.readout.title: Readout
rwkv.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then the head projects each position to 16 logits; the argmax at the answer positions recovers the copied tokens. The decaying-state recurrence has carried each data token forward at constant cost per step — no attention matrix, no growing cache.
scene.moe.title: MoE — selective copying
moe.ch.intro.title: The task
moe.ch.intro.body: A Mixture-of-Experts model holds many specialized feed-forward 'experts' but spends only a few on each token: a router scores the experts per token, keeps the top 2 of 4, and only those run — so the model's capacity grows without raising the per-token compute. Here a 2-layer model solves selective copying — recover 4 data tokens hidden among NOISE after a GO marker — using causal attention to move information between positions and routed experts to transform it. Every cell is a real value from a model that solves it at over 99% accuracy.
moe.ch.embed.title: Tokens + positions
moe.ch.embed.body: Each position adds a token embedding and a position embedding into the residual stream. The position signal is required because attention otherwise treats the sequence as an unordered set. The blocks then read and write this stream.
moe.ch.block.title: The block
moe.ch.block.body: An MoE block is a GPT block with one swap: the dense MLP becomes a mixture of experts. It still makes two passes over the residual stream — LayerNorm → causal self-attention (mix across positions), then LayerNorm → routed experts (transform each position). Both add a correction onto the stream. Follow the spine: ln1.out, attn.out, ln2.out, moe.out are the four stages of this first block.
moe.ch.qkv.title: Queries, keys, values
moe.ch.qkv.body: Attention begins by projecting the normalized stream into three roles per position — a query (what am I looking for?), a key (what do I offer?), a value (what would I pass on?). One matrix, qkv_proj, produces all three, then they are split across 3 heads of 16 dimensions each. q, k, v are shown as three-slab stacks, one slab per head.
moe.ch.scores.title: Attention scores
moe.ch.scores.body: Each head compares every query against every key by a scaled dot product, S = Q·Kᵀ/√d_head. Cell [i, j] is how strongly position i wants to read from position j. The strict upper triangle is set to −∞ so a position can never see the future — that is the dark causal-mask triangle.
moe.ch.weights.title: Softmax weights
moe.ch.weights.body: A softmax over each row turns scores into attention weights: non-negative, summing to 1 (the masked cells become 0). Watch the answer rows just after GO — their weight concentrates on the data-token positions back in the context. That bright band is the copy being set up.
moe.ch.attnout.title: Attention output
moe.ch.attnout.body: Each position takes a weighted average of the value vectors using its attention weights, so an answer position pulls in the value of the data token it attended to. The per-head results are concatenated, passed through out_proj, and added back onto the residual stream. This is the only stage that mixes across positions — everything after works per position.
moe.ch.router.title: The router
moe.ch.router.body: Here is what makes this an MoE. A small linear map (the router) scores the 4 experts for each token and softmaxes them — read moe.router, one row of expert affinities per position. Only the top 2 scores are kept and renormalized to sum to 1; the rest are zeroed. That is moe.gates: a per-token decision of which two experts will run and how much each contributes.
moe.ch.experts.title: The experts
moe.ch.experts.body: Each expert is its own MLP (fc → GELU → proj). All four are shown running on every token so you can compare them, but a token's actual output (moe.out) is the gate-weighted sum of only its top-2 experts — the other two contribute nothing to that token. That is the point of MoE: more experts add capacity without raising the per-token compute, because each token still runs only two.
moe.ch.layer2.title: The second block
moe.ch.layer2.body: The second block repeats the same two passes — attention then routed experts — on the now-updated stream, with its own router and its own four experts. A token may be routed to different experts here than in the first block. Deeper MoEs simply stack more of the identical block.
moe.ch.readout.title: Readout
moe.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then lm_head to 16 logits; the argmax at the answer positions recovers the copied tokens. Attention located each data token and the routed experts shaped the per-position representation that reads it out.
scene.kan.title: KAN — selective copying
kan.ch.intro.title: The task
kan.ch.intro.body: A Kolmogorov–Arnold network learns a function on every edge instead of a single weight. A standard MLP puts fixed activations on the nodes and learns the edge weights; a KAN replaces each weight with a learnable univariate function. This model uses the FastKAN form — every edge function is a learned sum of fixed Gaussian bumps. Here a 2-layer model solves selective copying — recover 4 data tokens hidden among NOISE after a GO marker — with causal attention to move information and a KAN as its feed-forward. Every cell is a real value from a model that solves the task at 100% accuracy.
kan.ch.embed.title: Tokens + positions
kan.ch.embed.body: Each position adds a token embedding and a position embedding into the residual stream. The position signal is needed because attention otherwise treats the sequence as an unordered set. The blocks then read and write this stream.
kan.ch.block.title: The block
kan.ch.block.body: A KAN block is a GPT block with one swap: the dense MLP becomes a Kolmogorov–Arnold network. It still makes two passes over the residual stream — LayerNorm → causal self-attention (mix across positions), then LayerNorm → KAN feed-forward (transform each position). Both add a correction onto the stream. Follow the spine: ln1.out, attn.out, ln2.out, kan.out are the four stages of this first block.
kan.ch.qkv.title: Queries, keys, values
kan.ch.qkv.body: Attention begins by projecting the normalized stream into three roles per position — a query (what am I looking for?), a key (what do I offer?), a value (what would I pass on?). One matrix, qkv_proj, produces all three, then they are split across 3 heads of 16 dimensions each. q, k, v are shown as three-slab stacks, one slab per head.
kan.ch.scores.title: Attention scores
kan.ch.scores.body: Each head compares every query against every key by a scaled dot product, S = Q·Kᵀ/√d_head. Cell [i, j] is how strongly position i wants to read from position j. The strict upper triangle is set to −∞ so a position can never see the future — that is the dark causal-mask triangle.
kan.ch.weights.title: Softmax weights
kan.ch.weights.body: A softmax over each row turns scores into attention weights: non-negative, summing to 1 (the masked cells become 0). Watch the answer rows just after GO — their weight concentrates on the data-token positions back in the context. That bright band is the copy being set up.
kan.ch.attnout.title: Attention output
kan.ch.attnout.body: Each position takes a weighted average of the value vectors using its attention weights, so an answer position pulls in the value of the data token it attended to. The per-head results are concatenated, passed through out_proj, and added back onto the residual stream. Attention does all the cross-position work — everything after this transforms each position on its own.
kan.ch.rbf.title: Gaussian basis
kan.ch.rbf.body: Here is what makes this a KAN. A standard MLP puts a fixed activation on each node and learns the edge weights; a KAN replaces each weight with a learnable univariate function. In the FastKAN form used here, every scalar of the normalized input (ln2.out, 48 values) is evaluated against 8 fixed Gaussian bumps spaced on a grid — exp(−((x̂−grid)/h)²). That is kan.rbf: 48 × 8 = 384 basis activations, a similarity profile of each input against the grid. It turns one number into a soft one-hot over where it falls.
kan.ch.spline.title: The learned edge functions
kan.ch.spline.body: The 384 basis activations are mixed by a learned linear map, spline_linear.weight, into the 48-dim output — spline = rbf · Wₛ. Because each output is a learned weighted sum of Gaussian bumps sampled at the input, each (input, output) edge realizes its own smooth 1-D function. This is the KAN idea made concrete: the expressiveness lives in the per-edge spline coefficients, not in a wide hidden layer.
kan.ch.kanout.title: Base path + sum
kan.ch.kanout.body: Alongside the spline, a base branch runs base = silu(x̂)·W_base — an ordinary linear layer with a SiLU activation, giving the block a smooth fallback that does not depend on the grid. The spline and base are summed into kan.out and added back onto the residual stream. The KAN feed-forward is the spline (learned edge functions) plus this base path.
kan.ch.layer2.title: The second block
kan.ch.layer2.body: The second block repeats the same two passes — attention then KAN feed-forward — on the now-updated stream, with its own attention weights and its own edge functions. It refines what the first block set up. Deeper KANs simply stack more of the identical block.
kan.ch.readout.title: Readout
kan.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then lm_head to 16 logits; the argmax recovers the copied tokens. Attention located each data token, and the learnable edge functions shaped the per-position representation that reads it out.
scene.diffusion.title: Diffusion — selective copying
diffusion.ch.intro.title: The task
diffusion.ch.intro.body: A text-diffusion model generates by denoising: corrupt a sequence by replacing tokens with a [MASK], train a bidirectional model to fill the blanks from the surrounding context, and at generation time start from all-masks and unmask step by step. Here a 2-layer denoiser solves selective copying as fill-in-the-blanks — the 4 answer positions after the GO marker are masked, and the model must recover the data tokens that were hidden earlier among NOISE. Every cell is a real value from a model that solves it at over 99% accuracy.
diffusion.ch.embed.title: Masked input + positions
diffusion.ch.embed.body: Each position adds a token embedding and a position embedding into the residual stream. The four answer positions are fed as a special [MASK] token — those are the blanks the denoiser must fill (look for the repeated rows at the tail of embed.out). Because a denoiser reads the whole sequence at once, the position signal must be supplied explicitly.
diffusion.ch.block.title: The block
diffusion.ch.block.body: The denoiser is built from Transformer blocks, the same two-pass shape as GPT — but its attention is bidirectional rather than causal. Each block does LayerNorm → self-attention (mix across positions), then LayerNorm → MLP (transform each position); both add a correction onto the residual stream. Follow the spine: ln1.out, attn.out, ln2.out, mlp.proj are the four stages of this first block.
diffusion.ch.qkv.title: Queries, keys, values
diffusion.ch.qkv.body: Attention begins by projecting the normalized stream into three roles per position — a query (what am I looking for?), a key (what do I offer?), a value (what would I pass on?). One matrix, qkv_proj, produces all three, then they are split across 3 heads of 16 dimensions each. q, k, v are shown as three-slab stacks, one slab per head — including at the masked answer positions, which query the rest of the sequence for their missing content.
diffusion.ch.scores.title: Bidirectional scores
diffusion.ch.scores.body: Each head compares every query against every key by a scaled dot product, S = Q·Kᵀ/√d_head. The decisive difference from GPT: there is NO causal mask — the scores have no dark triangle, every position attends to every other, including later ones. That bidirectional view is exactly what lets a masked answer position gather evidence from the data tokens, wherever they sit in the sequence.
diffusion.ch.weights.title: Softmax weights
diffusion.ch.weights.body: A softmax over each row turns scores into attention weights: non-negative, summing to 1. Look at the masked answer rows at the tail: their weight reaches back to the data-token positions earlier in the sequence — and, unlike a causal model, they could also look forward. The bright cells are the denoiser deciding which observed tokens fill each blank.
diffusion.ch.attnout.title: Attention output
diffusion.ch.attnout.body: Each position takes a weighted average of the value vectors using its attention weights — so a masked answer position pulls in the value of the data token it attended to. The per-head results are concatenated, passed through out_proj, and added back onto the residual stream. The evidence for each blank has now been gathered into the position that must fill it.
diffusion.ch.mlp.title: MLP
diffusion.ch.mlp.body: After attention mixes across positions, a per-position MLP (48 → 192 → 48, GELU) transforms each token on its own — no mixing across positions here. The wide 192-dim hidden layer is where most of the block's parameters live.
diffusion.ch.layer2.title: The second block
diffusion.ch.layer2.body: The second block repeats the same two passes — bidirectional attention then MLP — on the now-updated stream, with its own learned weights. It refines the evidence each masked position has gathered before readout. Deeper denoisers simply stack more of the identical block.
diffusion.ch.readout.title: Readout
diffusion.ch.readout.body: A final LayerNorm, then the head projects each position to 16 logits. At the masked answer positions the argmax already shows what the denoiser would fill in — a full guess for every blank in one pass. The next chapter turns that single pass into the real sampler.
diffusion.ch.sample.title: Iterative denoising
diffusion.ch.sample.body: A real sampler does not commit all the blanks at once. Watch it loop: step 0 has all four answer positions masked; each step commits the one masked position the model is most confident about, then re-runs the denoiser so the remaining blanks can use what was just revealed. Because attention is bidirectional, each newly filled token sharpens the others — the answer crystallizes in a few steps, then the loop restarts. Edit the input or hit Randomize and watch a different answer denoise.

ui.landing.title: Beyond Transformers
ui.landing.subtitle: A 3D, tensor-level tour of LLM architectures — real toy weights, trained and computed live in your browser. Open any architecture to see exactly how it works, cell by cell.
ui.input.label: Input
ui.input.hint: Click to change this data token (1–8)
compare.title: One task, six machines
compare.subtitle: Every architecture in this atlas is trained on the same selective-copying task. They differ only in how they move information between positions and how they transform it. Open any one to watch it run, cell by cell.
compare.mamba.idea: An input-dependent gate decides what the fixed-size state keeps or forgets.
compare.gpt.idea: Every position attends back over all earlier ones — the baseline Transformer.
compare.rwkv.idea: Attention rewritten as a decaying running state: trains in parallel, runs like an RNN.
compare.moe.idea: A router sends each token to a few experts — more capacity at the same per-token compute.
compare.kan.idea: Replaces the MLP's fixed activations with learned per-edge spline functions.
compare.diffusion.idea: Fills masked positions from both sides, denoising the answer over several steps.
card.mamba.title: Mamba
card.mamba.desc: Selective state space — a fixed-size recurrent state with an input-dependent gate.
card.gpt.title: GPT
card.rwkv.title: RWKV
card.rwkv.desc: A linear-attention recurrence with per-channel decay.
card.moe.title: Mixture of Experts
card.moe.desc: Sparse routing to a few expert MLPs per token.
card.kan.title: KAN
card.kan.desc: Kolmogorov–Arnold networks — learnable activations on edges.
card.diffusion.title: Text Diffusion
card.diffusion.desc: A bidirectional denoiser that generates by filling in masked tokens.
